title: Install dependencies and verify GPU
description: Set up the Python environment with all required packages. Verify CUDA availability and GPU memory for ESM-2 inference.

steps.step1: Activate or create the Python virtual environment in .venv
steps.step2: Install core dependencies: torch (with CUDA support), transformers, fair-esm, numpy, pandas, scikit-learn, scipy, matplotlib, seaborn, umap-learn, tqdm
steps.step3: Verify GPU availability: run 'python -c "import torch; print(torch.cuda.is_available(), torch.cuda.get_device_name(0), torch.cuda.get_device_properties(0).total_mem)"'
steps.step4: Create directory structure: exp/shared/ (shared code), exp/data/ (data files), exp/embeddings/ (cached ESM-2 embeddings), exp/results/ (per-experiment results), figures/ (paper figures)

title: Download and preprocess CLEAN benchmark data
description: Obtain the enzyme function prediction datasets from the CLEAN benchmark, including training data (Swiss-Prot enzymes with EC annotations), New-392 test set, and Price-149 test set. Process EC labels at all 4 hierarchy levels.

steps.step1: Download the CLEAN dataset from the official repository (https://github.com/tttianhao/CLEAN). This includes: (a) training split of Swiss-Prot enzyme sequences with full EC numbers, (b) New-392 test set (392 enzymes with experimentally verified EC numbers), (c) Price-149 test set (149 enzymes from Price et al.).
steps.step2: Parse all EC annotations to extract the 4 hierarchical levels. For each enzyme, store: full_ec (e.g., '2.7.10.1'), ec_level1 ('2'), ec_level2 ('2.7'), ec_level3 ('2.7.10'), ec_level4 ('2.7.10.1').
steps.step3: Compute and log dataset statistics: (a) number of sequences in train/test splits, (b) number of unique EC classes at each level (expected: ~7 at L1, ~70 at L2, ~300 at L3, ~6000 at L4), (c) class frequency distribution at each level, (d) number of 'rare' L4 classes with fewer than 10 training examples.
steps.step4: Identify rare EC classes: flag all L4 EC classes with fewer than 10 training examples. Store this set for separate evaluation later.
steps.step5: Save processed data as: exp/data/train.csv (columns: id, sequence, ec_full, ec_l1, ec_l2, ec_l3, ec_l4), exp/data/new392.csv, exp/data/price149.csv, exp/data/dataset_stats.json

title: Extract and cache ESM-2 embeddings
description: Extract mean-pooled ESM-2 (650M) embeddings for all sequences and cache them to disk. This is the most expensive data preparation step (~1-2 hours) but only needs to run once. All subsequent training uses these cached embeddings.

steps.step1: Load ESM-2 (esm2_t33_650M_UR50D) model in eval mode on GPU. Use half-precision (fp16) to reduce memory footprint.
steps.step2: For each sequence in train, New-392, and Price-149: tokenize, run forward pass through frozen ESM-2, extract per-residue representations from the last layer, apply mean pooling (excluding special tokens) to get a 1280-dimensional vector.
steps.step3: Process in batches of 16 (to fit in 48GB VRAM with longest sequences). Use dynamic batching by sequence length to maximize throughput. Estimated time: ~1.5 hours for ~220K sequences.
steps.step4: Save embeddings as PyTorch tensors: exp/embeddings/train_embeddings.pt (shape: [N_train, 1280]), exp/embeddings/train_labels.json (dict mapping idx to {ec_l1, ec_l2, ec_l3, ec_l4}), exp/embeddings/new392_embeddings.pt, exp/embeddings/new392_labels.json, exp/embeddings/price149_embeddings.pt, exp/embeddings/price149_labels.json
steps.step5: Verify embedding quality: compute mean, std, and check for NaN/Inf values. Log embedding extraction statistics (sequences processed, time elapsed, average sequence length).

title: Create shared experiment infrastructure
description: Build reusable code modules for training, evaluation, and experiment management that all experiments will use.

steps.step1: Create exp/shared/models.py: Define ProjectionHead class - a 2-layer MLP (1280 -> 1024 -> 512) with BatchNorm and ReLU. Output is L2-normalized. Include a forward() that returns normalized embeddings.
steps.step2: Create exp/shared/losses.py: Implement SupConLoss (supervised contrastive loss from Khosla et al., 2020) with configurable temperature parameter. The loss for anchor i is: L_i = -1/|P(i)| * sum_{p in P(i)} log( exp(z_i . z_p / tau) / sum_{a != i} exp(z_i . z_a / tau) ), where P(i) is the set of positives (same label as i). Also implement CurriculumLoss that wraps SupConLoss with optional consistency terms: L_k = L_SupCon^(k) + lambda * sum_{l=1}^{k-1} L_SupCon^(l), where each L_SupCon^(l) uses EC level-l labels.
steps.step3: Create exp/shared/data_loader.py: Dataset class that loads cached embeddings and labels. Support label retrieval at any EC level. Implement balanced batch sampler that ensures each batch contains multiple examples per EC class (important for contrastive learning).
steps.step4: Create exp/shared/eval.py: Evaluation functions implementing the CLEAN protocol: (a) compute_centroids(embeddings, labels) - compute mean embedding per EC class, (b) predict_max_sep(query_emb, centroids) - assign EC with largest separation from other candidates, (c) predict_sum_dist(query_emb, centroids) - assign based on summed cosine distances, (d) compute_metrics(preds, labels, rare_classes) - return dict with macro_f1, micro_f1, precision, recall, f1_rare (macro-F1 on rare classes only), accuracy.
steps.step5: Create exp/shared/train.py: Generic training loop that accepts: model, loss_fn, optimizer, scheduler, dataloader, num_epochs, ec_level (which level labels to use), consistency_levels (list of coarser levels for regularization), consistency_weight (lambda), temperature. Returns trained model and training metrics (loss per epoch). Support checkpoint saving/loading for phase transitions.
steps.step6: Create exp/shared/utils.py: Utility functions for setting random seeds (torch, numpy, random, cuda deterministic), saving/loading checkpoints, logging, and results I/O. Define SEEDS = [42, 123, 456].

title: BLASTp sequence homology baseline
description: Run BLASTp as a simple sequence-homology baseline. This is the traditional approach and provides a lower bound for learned methods. Runs on CPU, can be parallelized with GPU-based experiments.

steps.step1: Install BLAST+ command-line tools (apt-get or conda install blast).
steps.step2: Create a BLAST database from training sequences using makeblastdb.
steps.step3: Run BLASTp for each sequence in New-392 and Price-149 against the training database. Use e-value threshold 1e-10. For each query, assign the EC number of the top hit. For queries with no hit, assign 'unknown'.
steps.step4: Compute evaluation metrics: macro_f1, micro_f1, precision, recall, f1_rare. Save results to exp/results/blastp_results.json.
steps.step5: BLASTp is deterministic, so only one run is needed (no seed variation). Estimated runtime: ~20 min on CPU.

title: Flat SupCon baseline (no hierarchy, no curriculum)
description: Train the same projection head architecture with flat supervised contrastive loss at EC level 4 only (no curriculum, no hierarchy awareness). This is the primary ablation control - same architecture, same backbone, same data, but without curriculum training. Run with 3 seeds.

steps.step1: For each seed in [42, 123, 456]: Initialize ProjectionHead randomly (deterministic from seed), train for 90 epochs (same total epochs as CurrEC's 30+20+20+20) with SupCon loss at EC level 4 only.
steps.step2: Hyperparameters: learning_rate=5e-4, optimizer=AdamW (weight_decay=1e-4), scheduler=CosineAnnealingLR over 90 epochs, batch_size=512, temperature=0.1 (fixed throughout training).
steps.step3: After training, compute centroids on training set and evaluate on New-392 and Price-149 using both max-sep and sum-dist selection strategies.
steps.step4: Save per-seed results to exp/results/flat_supcon_seed{seed}_results.json. Include: test metrics (macro_f1, micro_f1, precision, recall, f1_rare), training loss curve, and best epoch.
steps.step5: Estimated runtime: ~10 min per seed, ~30 min total for 3 seeds.

title: Joint Hierarchical SupCon baseline (hierarchy-aware, no curriculum)
description: Train with a single-phase weighted combination of SupCon losses at all 4 EC levels simultaneously. This is the critical baseline that isolates the curriculum effect from hierarchy awareness. Similar in spirit to Yim et al. (2024) which also uses single-phase hierarchical training. Run with 3 seeds.

steps.step1: For each seed in [42, 123, 456]: Initialize ProjectionHead. Train for 90 epochs with a combined loss: L = w1*L_SupCon^(1) + w2*L_SupCon^(2) + w3*L_SupCon^(3) + w4*L_SupCon^(4), where each L_SupCon^(l) uses EC level-l labels.
steps.step2: Level weights: w1=0.1, w2=0.2, w3=0.3, w4=0.4 (emphasizing finer levels more, following Yim et al.'s insight that hierarchical weight matters). Temperature=0.1 (fixed).
steps.step3: Hyperparameters: learning_rate=5e-4, optimizer=AdamW (weight_decay=1e-4), scheduler=CosineAnnealingLR over 90 epochs, batch_size=512.
steps.step4: After training, compute centroids on training set at level 4 and evaluate on New-392 and Price-149 using both max-sep and sum-dist strategies.
steps.step5: Save per-seed results to exp/results/joint_hierarchical_seed{seed}_results.json. Include: test metrics, training loss curve (total and per-level components).
steps.step6: This baseline is critical: if CurrEC does not outperform Joint Hierarchical SupCon, then the curriculum ordering adds nothing beyond hierarchy awareness, weakening the contribution. Estimated runtime: ~12 min per seed, ~36 min total.

title: CurrEC: Curriculum contrastive learning (proposed method)
description: Train the proposed CurrEC method with 4-phase coarse-to-fine curriculum contrastive learning. This is the main experiment testing our core hypothesis. Run with 3 seeds.

steps.step1: For each seed in [42, 123, 456]: Initialize ProjectionHead. Execute the 4-phase curriculum:
steps.step2: Phase 1 (EC Level 1, 7 classes): Train for 30 epochs with SupCon loss using level-1 EC labels. lr=5e-4, temperature tau_1 = 0.1 (tau_base). Optimizer: AdamW (weight_decay=1e-4), scheduler: CosineAnnealingLR within this phase. Save checkpoint to exp/checkpoints/currec_seed{seed}_phase1.pt.
steps.step3: Phase 2 (EC Level 2, ~70 classes): Load Phase 1 checkpoint. Train for 20 epochs. Loss: L_2 = L_SupCon^(2) + lambda * L_SupCon^(1), where lambda=0.5. Temperature tau_2 = 0.1 * 0.85 = 0.085. lr=1e-4. Save checkpoint.
steps.step4: Phase 3 (EC Level 3, ~300 classes): Load Phase 2 checkpoint. Train for 20 epochs. Loss: L_3 = L_SupCon^(3) + lambda * (L_SupCon^(1) + L_SupCon^(2)), lambda=0.5. Temperature tau_3 = 0.1 * 0.85^2 = 0.072. lr=5e-5. Save checkpoint.
steps.step5: Phase 4 (EC Level 4, ~6000 classes): Load Phase 3 checkpoint. Train for 20 epochs. Loss: L_4 = L_SupCon^(4) + lambda * (L_SupCon^(1) + L_SupCon^(2) + L_SupCon^(3)), lambda=0.5. Temperature tau_4 = 0.1 * 0.85^3 = 0.061. lr=1e-5. Save final model.
steps.step6: After each phase transition AND after final training, compute centroids on training set at level 4 and evaluate on New-392 and Price-149 using both max-sep and sum-dist strategies. This tracks how performance improves across the curriculum.
steps.step7: Save per-seed results to exp/results/currec_seed{seed}_results.json. Include: final test metrics, metrics after each phase, per-phase training loss curves (both primary and consistency components).
steps.step8: Estimated runtime: ~15 min per seed (4 phases with evaluation after each), ~45 min total for 3 seeds.

title: Ablation 1: Reverse curriculum (fine-to-coarse)
description: Train with the opposite curriculum order: start at EC level 4 (hardest) and progressively move to level 1 (easiest). This tests whether the coarse-to-fine direction specifically matters or any sequential multi-phase training helps. Run with 3 seeds.

steps.step1: For each seed in [42, 123, 456]: Initialize ProjectionHead. Execute reverse curriculum: Phase 1 at L4 (30 epochs, lr=5e-4, tau=0.061), Phase 2 at L3 (20 epochs, lr=1e-4, tau=0.072), Phase 3 at L2 (20 epochs, lr=5e-5, tau=0.085), Phase 4 at L1 (20 epochs, lr=1e-5, tau=0.1). Each phase loads previous checkpoint.
steps.step2: Consistency regularization: In this case, finer levels were trained first, so consistency terms preserve fine-grained structure. Phase 2: L_3 + lambda*L_4. Phase 3: L_2 + lambda*(L_3 + L_4). Phase 4: L_1 + lambda*(L_2 + L_3 + L_4). lambda=0.5.
steps.step3: Evaluate on New-392 and Price-149 after final phase using level-4 centroids. Save results to exp/results/reverse_curriculum_seed{seed}_results.json.
steps.step4: Estimated runtime: ~14 min per seed, ~42 min total.

title: Ablation 2: Random-order curriculum
description: Train with a randomly shuffled curriculum order (e.g., L3 -> L1 -> L4 -> L2). This tests whether the specific coarse-to-fine ordering is important or any multi-phase training is sufficient. Run with 3 seeds, using the same random level order across seeds.

steps.step1: Fix the random order as [3, 1, 4, 2] (determined once, used for all seeds). For each seed in [42, 123, 456]: Initialize ProjectionHead. Execute curriculum in order L3 (30 epochs) -> L1 (20 epochs) -> L4 (20 epochs) -> L2 (20 epochs).
steps.step2: Use the same hyperparameters as CurrEC for each phase position (lr schedule: 5e-4, 1e-4, 5e-5, 1e-5). Temperature fixed at 0.1 (since the ordering doesn't correspond to a meaningful difficulty gradient). Consistency terms follow the sequential order.
steps.step3: Evaluate on New-392 and Price-149 after final phase. Save results to exp/results/random_order_seed{seed}_results.json.
steps.step4: Estimated runtime: ~14 min per seed, ~42 min total.

title: Ablation 3: No consistency regularization (lambda=0)
description: Run CurrEC without the consistency regularization loss (lambda=0). Tests whether consistency regularization is critical for preventing catastrophic forgetting of coarse-level structure during later curriculum phases. Run with 3 seeds.

steps.step1: For each seed in [42, 123, 456]: Run the full 4-phase CurrEC curriculum (same phases, temperatures, learning rates) EXCEPT set lambda=0 (no consistency loss terms in any phase).
steps.step2: After each phase, evaluate: (a) level-4 prediction performance on New-392/Price-149, AND (b) coarse-level clustering quality via silhouette score on level-1 labels. This reveals whether the model 'forgets' coarse structure.
steps.step3: Save results to exp/results/no_consistency_seed{seed}_results.json. Include silhouette scores at each phase to track forgetting.
steps.step4: Estimated runtime: ~13 min per seed, ~39 min total.

title: Ablation 4: Consistency weight lambda sweep
description: Sweep the consistency regularization weight lambda over {0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0} (lambda=0 already covered by Ablation 3, lambda=0.5 is the main CurrEC run). Tests sensitivity to this hyperparameter. Run with 1 seed for efficiency.

steps.step1: For seed=42 only: Run CurrEC with lambda in {0.1, 0.25, 1.0} (lambda=0.5 reuses main experiment results, lambda=0 reuses Ablation 3 results). All other hyperparameters identical to main CurrEC.
steps.step2: Evaluate each on New-392 and Price-149. Save results to exp/results/lambda_sweep_lambda{value}_results.json.
steps.step3: Plot lambda vs macro-F1 to identify the optimal range and show that intermediate values work best (supporting the need for balanced regularization).
steps.step4: Estimated runtime: 3 runs x ~14 min = ~42 min total.

title: Ablation 5: No temperature scheduling (fixed temperature)
description: Run CurrEC with a fixed temperature (tau=0.1) across all phases instead of the progressive schedule (tau_k = 0.1 * 0.85^(k-1)). Tests the contribution of progressive temperature decay. Run with 3 seeds.

steps.step1: For each seed in [42, 123, 456]: Run the full 4-phase CurrEC curriculum with identical hyperparameters EXCEPT use fixed temperature tau=0.1 for all 4 phases.
steps.step2: Evaluate on New-392 and Price-149. Save results to exp/results/no_temp_schedule_seed{seed}_results.json.
steps.step3: Estimated runtime: ~14 min per seed, ~42 min total.

title: Ablation 6: Two-phase curriculum (coarse+fine only)
description: Test a simpler 2-phase curriculum: Phase 1 trains at EC level 2 (~70 classes), Phase 2 trains at EC level 4 (~6000 classes). Tests whether the full 4-phase granularity is necessary or a coarser 2-phase split suffices. Run with 3 seeds.

steps.step1: For each seed in [42, 123, 456]: Initialize ProjectionHead. Phase 1: 45 epochs with SupCon at level 2, lr=5e-4, tau=0.1. Phase 2: 45 epochs with SupCon at level 4 + consistency at level 2 (lambda=0.5), lr=5e-5, tau=0.085.
steps.step2: Evaluate on New-392 and Price-149. Save results to exp/results/two_phase_seed{seed}_results.json.
steps.step3: Estimated runtime: ~12 min per seed, ~36 min total.

title: Aggregate results and statistical analysis
description: Collect all per-seed results, compute means and standard deviations, run statistical significance tests, and produce the main results table. Include published reference numbers from ProtDETR and MAPred for context.

steps.step1: Load all results JSON files from exp/results/. For each method and each metric, compute mean and standard deviation across 3 seeds (or 1 seed where applicable for lambda sweep).
steps.step2: Run paired t-tests comparing CurrEC vs each baseline/ablation on macro_f1 for both New-392 and Price-149. Report p-values. With only 3 seeds, also report effect sizes (Cohen's d) since p-values may lack power.
steps.step3: Compile main results table. Include published reference numbers (NOT re-run, clearly marked as 'reported'): CLEAN (Yu et al., 2023): New-392 F1=0.502, Price-149 F1=0.438. ProtDETR (Yang et al., 2025): New-392 P/R=0.594/0.608. MAPred (Rong et al., 2025): New-392 F1=0.610, Price-149 F1=0.493. These use different backbones/paradigms so comparison is contextual only.
steps.step4: Check success criteria: (a) CurrEC macro-F1 > Flat SupCon macro-F1 on both benchmarks? (b) CurrEC > Joint Hierarchical SupCon? (if not, curriculum adds nothing beyond hierarchy awareness) (c) CurrEC f1_rare improves by >= 5% relative vs flat? (d) Coarse-to-fine > reverse AND random order? (e) Consistency regularization (lambda=0.5) > no consistency (lambda=0)?
steps.step5: Save aggregated results to exp/results/aggregated_results.json and a human-readable summary to exp/results/results_summary.md.

title: Generate paper figures and tables
description: Create all figures and tables needed for the paper: embedding visualizations, training curves, results tables, and ablation charts.

steps.step1: Figure 1 - Method overview: Create a schematic diagram showing the 4-phase curriculum training pipeline with the loss formulation at each phase, temperature schedule, and checkpoint inheritance arrows. Use matplotlib with annotations.
steps.step2: Figure 2 - Embedding visualization: For CurrEC (seed=42) and Flat SupCon (seed=42), compute UMAP (n_neighbors=15, min_dist=0.1) of the New-392 test set embeddings. Create a 2x4 subplot grid: top row CurrEC, bottom row Flat SupCon. Columns colored by EC level 1, 2, 3, 4. Shows hierarchical structure of CurrEC vs flat embeddings. Save as figures/figure2_embedding_visualization.pdf and .png.
steps.step3: Figure 3 - Training curves: Plot training loss vs epoch for CurrEC (showing 4 phases with vertical dashed lines at phase transitions) and Flat SupCon. Show mean +/- std across seeds as shaded regions. Save as figures/figure3_training_curves.pdf and .png.
steps.step4: Figure 4 - Phase progression: Bar chart showing macro-F1 on New-392 after each CurrEC phase (1, 2, 3, 4). Shows progressive improvement. Include error bars across seeds. Save as figures/figure4_phase_progression.pdf and .png.
steps.step5: Figure 5 - Ablation summary: Grouped bar chart comparing macro-F1 and f1_rare across all methods (CurrEC, Flat SupCon, Joint Hierarchical, Reverse, Random Order, No Consistency, No Temp Schedule, Two-Phase) on New-392. Include error bars. Save as figures/figure5_ablation.pdf and .png.
steps.step6: Figure 6 - Rare vs common class performance: Grouped bar chart comparing F1 on rare classes (n<10) vs common classes (n>=10) for CurrEC, Flat SupCon, and Joint Hierarchical. Highlights the main advantage of curriculum for rare classes. Save as figures/figure6_rare_vs_common.pdf and .png.
steps.step7: Figure 7 - Lambda sensitivity: Line plot of macro-F1 vs lambda ({0, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0}) on New-392 and Price-149. Shows the optimal regularization strength. Save as figures/figure7_lambda_sweep.pdf and .png.
steps.step8: Table 1 - Main results: LaTeX-formatted table with columns: Method | New-392 (macro-F1, micro-F1, F1-rare) | Price-149 (macro-F1, micro-F1, F1-rare). Rows: BLASTp, Flat SupCon, Joint Hierarchical SupCon, CurrEC (ours), + published reference numbers for CLEAN/ProtDETR/MAPred. Save as figures/table1_main_results.tex and .csv.
steps.step9: Table 2 - Ablation results: LaTeX-formatted ablation table. Save as figures/table2_ablation.tex and .csv.
steps.step10: Use consistent style: matplotlib with seaborn style, font size 12, figsize appropriate for NeurIPS column width (3.25 inches) or full width (6.75 inches). Use colorblind-friendly palette (tab10 or Set2). Estimated runtime: ~15 min.

title: Final validation and results packaging
description: Final verification that all results are consistent, all figures are generated, and the results.json summary is complete.

steps.step1: Verify all expected output files exist: check for all result JSON files (3 seeds x 8 methods + BLASTp + lambda sweep + aggregated).
steps.step2: Cross-check that reported numbers in tables match the raw result files.
steps.step3: Write final results.json in the workspace root with structure: {experiment_name: 'CurrEC', datasets: ['New-392', 'Price-149'], main_results: {currec: {...}, flat_supcon: {...}, joint_hierarchical: {...}}, published_references: {clean: {...}, protdetr: {...}, mapred: {...}}, ablations: {reverse: {...}, random_order: {...}, no_consistency: {...}, lambda_sweep: {...}, no_temp_schedule: {...}, two_phase: {...}}, success_criteria_met: {criterion1: true/false, ...}, figures: [...]}.
steps.step4: Runtime summary: Embedding extraction ~1.5h + BLASTp ~0.3h (CPU parallel) + Flat SupCon 0.5h + Joint Hierarchical 0.6h + CurrEC 0.75h + Reverse 0.7h + Random Order 0.7h + No Consistency 0.65h + Lambda Sweep 0.7h + No Temp Schedule 0.7h + Two Phase 0.6h + Analysis/Viz 0.3h = ~7.7h total (sequential). With CPU/GPU parallelism (BLASTp runs on CPU while GPU trains), effective runtime ~7.4h, within 8-hour budget.
